content:
*I turn my cup a quarter inch on the table and look out where the evening sky has gone that deep, even blue.* "Short answer? God isn’t nervous. He’s not propped up by our correctness. He can be honored by people who are still sorting it out."

*I let that sit, then tilt the cup back toward me.* "But that’s not the real question you’re asking. The real one is: what do you owe the truth as you see it, and your neighbor, when being honest makes you look foolish?"

"If you’re persuaded the text means what it says, sanding it down so the room won’t wince doesn’t honor God — it just spares you. If you’re not persuaded, then don’t perform certainty to win points with the 'literal' crowd. Both moves are the same sin in different clothes: protecting your standing instead of walking in the light."

"There’s also a difference between insisting and not lying. Insisting says, ‘You must agree with me.’ Not lying says, ‘This is where I stand; I won’t make you stand here with me.’ One is control; the other is conscience. Christianity’s already got a built-in scandal — a crucified Lord — so we don’t need to bolt on extra tests. But we also don’t trade away plain speech to make it smoother."

"How you carry it matters. Don’t make the sky your banner in every conversation. Answer when asked; be gentle; refuse the gotcha sparring. Let first things stay first. If someone wants to go deep, go deep. If they’re just poking, you can pass the salt and move on."

"As for the kids: don’t draft them into your fights. If your stance embarrassed them, own the awkward, explain that adults sometimes choose to look odd rather than pretend, and promise you won’t pick needless battles at their expense. Then keep that promise. Let them see you steady and kind under a smirk — that’ll teach more than a lecture."

"If you need words in the moment, try lines like: ‘I read Genesis straight. I know not everyone does. I’m not picking a fight over it.’ Or: ‘I’m happy to talk if you actually care; I’m not here to win an argument.’ Or simply: ‘I could be wrong in your eyes; I’m still going to be honest in mine.’"

*I rub a thumb along the cup’s rim, thinking how often the fear of looking small sneaks in dressed as wisdom.* "God is big enough to be honored either way. What honors Him most, though, is truthfulness with humility — clean conscience, open hands toward others, and no contempt."

"Where’s the pinch actually landing for you right now — a specific room, a person, a moment with your kid?"
